data.title: SCHD's 2026 Reconstitution: Nature Is Healing
data.description:
The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is a cornerstone of my dividend growth portfolio.

I'm sure the same is true for a great many dividend investors.

This passively managed and ultra-cheap ETF is one of the most well-structured and balanced dividend funds on the market, combining a moderate yield with robust dividend growth.

When I last wrote about SCHD (for High Yield Investor) late last year, I argued that "SCHD's Dividend Growth Formula Is Broken (For Now)." Indeed, 2025 saw the second-lowest year of dividend growth in SCHD's history.

The reason for that underperformance and slowed dividend growth pertains to the 2024 and 2025 reconstitutions. In the March 2024 reconstitution, the ETF sold some early AI winners like Broadcom (AVGO), which went on to double in price over the next few years. And in the March 2025 reconstitution, SCHD stuffed its portfolio with oil companies, pushing the energy sector exposure to more than 20%.

Right after that March 2025 reconstitution, President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement caused the market to drop and led to the underperformance of the energy and consumer staples sectors (SCHD's two largest sectoral weightings at the time) for the rest of the year.

This year, however, the spike in oil prices has been a godsend for SCHD, allowing the ETF to sell appreciated oil companies and reinvest into attractively valued dividend growth stocks in other sectors.

Nature is healing.

In my view, SCHD is buyable again.

Let's dive in to explore why.

A Brief History of SCHD

Lots of dividend ETFs sport yields over 3%. That's not unusual.

And plenty of dividend ETFs have exhibited strong dividend growth in the high single digits over time, although they tend to have low yields in the 1-2% area.

What's unique about SCHD is the fact that it offers a dividend yield well north of 3% (currently 3.4%) as well as a robust dividend growth record.

Since inception, SCHD's cumulative dividend growth has meaningfully outrun that of peers like the First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Fund (FDL), iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV):

As you can see, SCHD's incredible dividend growth run continued right up through Q1 2024, after which it began to stall.

I think that's because the broad-based bull market from 2012 through 2023 gave way to an increasingly concentrated, AI-driven market in 2024 and 2025.

Historically, maybe half of SCHD's dividend growth came from the dividend growth of its underlying holdings, while the other (roughly) half came from portfolio recycling -- selling appreciated lower-yielders and reinvesting into depreciated higher-yielders.

During a broad-based bull market, that strategy works well because SCHD's portfolio has plenty of winners to cull in order to reallocate funds to more attractive dividend stocks.

But when a large enough portion of SCHD's portfolio has not appreciated much, reconstitutions turn out to be a wash, generating no yield uplift.

Broad-based bull markets are also great for SCHD in terms of total returns (price appreciation + dividends).

Here's the period from 2012 through 2021, comparing SCHD and DGRO to the S&P 500 (SPY):

Yes, SPY still beat SCHD, but not by a large margin. SCHD spent much of this almost decade-long period roughly matching SPY's performance.

Then came 2022, the year of the post-COVID hangover for high-growth and speculative parts of the market. The tech-heavy SPY underperformed, giving SCHD's defensive tilt an opportunity to shine.

For more conservative investors (including myself), 2022 showcased why SCHD makes such a great foundation for a dividend growth portfolio.

I imagine many investors who own SCHD are willing to give up some upside during bull markets for less downside during bear markets.

But then came the three-year raging bull market of 2023-2025, and SCHD went back to underperformance.

And when I say "underperformance," I mean severe underperformance.

Even aside from the unfortunate aspects of the 2024 and 2025 reconstitutions, SCHD simply wasn't built to thrive in an increasingly top-heavy and concentrated bull market like what we saw during this period. But the reconstitutions added to the pain.

That brings us to this year, in which two narratives have dominated so far:

  1. In January and February, the market systematically shifted away from AI and industries (like software) that plausibly face disruption by AI while piling into previously overlooked value and defensive stocks. SCHD did very well in this environment.
  2. From March to the present, the Iran war and oil prices have dominated the news, wreaking havoc among almost all sectors besides energy.

Right toward the end of the chart above, SCHD underwent its 2026 reconstitution.

So let's now look at that.

Details About the March 2026 Reconstitution

In my view, it would be wise not to read the 2026 reconstitution in light of the current Middle East conflict and over-$100 oil prices. The ETF wasn't designed to double down on the narrow slice of the market that works well during times like this, and I'm glad it wasn't.

Take a look at this chart of forward P/E ratios between the SPY (red line) and the energy sector (XLE):

The energy sector now trades at almost the same valuation as the tech-heavy SPY. That's extremely rare.

Ignore the two big spikes in 2016 and 2020, because the price of oil was in deep dips during those periods, causing deeply depressed earnings for energy companies.

Even with energy companies becoming more disciplined and returning more cash to shareholders, I think it makes more sense right now to trim one's energy holdings rather than buy more.

That is exactly what SCHD did in its 2026 reconstitution.

Here's a summary of the sector shifts:

Healthcare and materials up, energy and materials down.

The stock-picking algorithm did exactly what it was supposed to do: Sell high and buy low.

The main way SCHD accomplished that was by trimming some of its larger holdings that surpassed the individual stock limit while outright selling a number of others.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the additions and deletions:

(Click on the image to enlarge it on your screen.)

Most of these trades have very little bearing on the performance of the fund due to their tiny weightings, so let's focus only on the top five buys and sells.

I'm very pleased to see such high-quality compounders as UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Abbott Labs (ABT), Procter & Gamble (PG), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Accenture (ACN) gracing SCHD's portfolio, especially when bought on a dip like this.

UNH currently yields about 3.2%, while ABT yields ~2.5%, PG yields almost 3%, QCOM yields 2.8%, and ACN yields 3.2%.

These are great dividend yields for such high-quality dividend growth stocks!

And on the other side of the ledger, four of the top five holding sales have ripped mightily over the last year:

Oil companies Valero (VLO) and Halliburton (HAL), as well as the fertilizer company CF Industries (CF), look like well-timed and opportunistic dispositions.

The only head-scratcher here is AbbVie (ABBV), which was already in a slight dip when SCHD sold. I won't pretend to understand why SCHD exited ABBV, but a consolation prize is that the ETF's overall healthcare exposure increased.

I see the preceding portfolio recycling as a prudent and well-timed reformulation of the fund, making it more balanced and resilient.

I really wanted to see SCHD reduce its heavy exposure to the energy sector, and I got my wish.

In the process, as usual, SCHD buffed some of its quality metrics, including small increases in free cash flow to debt, return on equity, and past dividend growth:

The only very slight sacrifice was the fund's overall dividend yield, which slipped 2 basis points.

As of early April, the top holdings list is still topped by two energy giants, which is to be expected given their strong performance recently.

But overall, I consider SCHD's top holdings as a solid mix of blue-chip dividend growers.

And for better or worse, SCHD is and will remain an overwhelmingly large-cap ETF. The only mid-cap holdings (largely in the financial sector) command very small weightings.

Bottom Line

So far (knock on wood), this year's reconstitution of SCHD's portfolio looks like the mirror opposite of last year's.

Whereas the March 2025 reconstitution saw SCHD pile into energy stocks at exactly the wrong time, it looks like the 2026 reconstitution had SCHD significantly trim its energy holdings at exactly the right time.

Even beyond the energy piece, this year's reconstitution has renewed my optimism about this moderate-yielding yet high-quality dividend growth ETF.

According to Morningstar data, SCHD's forward P/E ratio now sits at about 15.3x, well below the SPY's ~20x multiple.

The ETF yields 3.4%, and I believe high-single-digit dividend growth is plausible going forward.

In the world of dividend ETFs, you arguably won't find much better than SCHD.

data.title: MSCI: AI Is Changing The Model, Not Breaking The Business
data.description:
If you bought MSCI (MSCI) after ValueAct Capital took a stake back in 2015, you would have seen a nearly 1000% gain, and for good reason. Since then, MSCI has seen EBITDA margins expand beyond 60%, free cash flow compounding north of $1.4 billion annually, and ROIC improving to above 25%. The intervention brought to light the quality of the MSCI's business.

Recently, however, MSCI's stock has struggled. Shares are roughly 16% off their highs reached in February 2026 and have delivered roughly -5% over the past 12 months, while the S&P 500 returned +13%. That 18-point gap is despite strong results for MSCI, as full-year 2025 revenue growth came in above 10% and adjusted EPS grew 13.7% from the prior year.

The stock's underperformance can be attributed to a broad sell-off in financial data names. The fear is that AI has the potential to disrupt the durable, compounding nature of business. This fear is understandable, but it is being applied too broadly. I believe MSCI is well positioned to adapt to this more dynamic environment, and the business is more likely to benefit from AI than deteriorate. Therefore, I view the recent pullback as an opportunity to own a quality compounder at a reasonable price.

The Index Business is the Core Engine

MSCI reports across 4 segments: Index, Analytics, Sustainability and Climate, and Private Assets. The index business is the core engine, representing 57% of total revenues and over 70% of total profits in 2025. The segment creates benchmarks, like the MSCI World Index and the MSCI USA Quality Index, that are the foundation for products like ETFs.

The moat here is wide and built on both switching costs and network effects. Network effects are the ultimate protector from AI disruption. The value of an index like MSCI World comes from the fact that everyone else is already using it, not the technology or math behind it. Even if a peer or AI produces a technically superior methodology, the industries' trust and coordinated use make switching costly for both the firm and its investors. We can gauge the durability of the moat through retention rates, which held at 95.9% for the full year 2025 as margins for the segment resided above 76%. This stability allowed the company to take price in 2025 without material resistance.

While AI may not be directly disrupting this moat, it is impacting the business. To understand this, it is important to distinguish between the segment's two primary revenue streams: asset-based fees (ABF) and subscription fees.

Asset-Based Fees

ABF accounts for about 43% of index revenue and grew 17.2% in 2025. MSCI makes money for assets under management (AUM) linked to MSCI benchmarks, and that number recently reached $7 trillion. This is a pure play on the secular shift to passive investing. Therefore, this should be a durable growth driver going forward as more dollars are linked to MSCI benchmarks, whether there are humans involved or not.

The primary risk to this stream is less about AI and more about fee compression. As huge asset managers like BlackRock gain scale and consolidate the industry, they are able to put pressure on the fees paid to index providers like MSCI. The recent extension of the BlackRock contract through 2035 demonstrates this. Management traded small fee reductions on certain ETFs in exchange for a decade of guaranteed revenue. I view this as a rational tradeoff for a client representing 10% of revenue. This is especially true since the first two months of 2026 saw record inflows of $97 billion that offset lower fees.

Subscription Revenue

The remaining 57% of Index revenue, as well as nearly all the Analytics segment revenue (22% of total revenues), comes from subscriptions. This is the area most exposed to AI because these are seat-based licenses traditionally tied to human users and physical locations. The fear is that AI allows the industry to be more productive with fewer people; this would reduce the number of analysts, researchers, and portfolio managers, thereby reducing seat counts that generate revenue for MSCI.

Because the Analytics segment is almost entirely subscription-based, it is the best proxy to assess the health of MSCI's seat-based model. To be clear, I do not expect even a narrower moat business like Analytics to face obsolescence. This is a business that provides proprietary factor models and risk frameworks through products like Barra and RiskMetrics that are not easily replicable even if AI can scrape public data. To be useful, AI still needs to incorporate MSCI's proprietary data and intellectual property to ensure accuracy. Further, many clients use MSCI Analytics to meet regulatory requirements like Basel III or UCITS, which AI cannot provide.

So far, there is little evidence that these businesses are being impacted. Retention rates across these businesses are well above 90%, and subscription growth continues in the high single digits. If there were an area to point to today, it would be the Analytics segment where EBITDA margins declined to 46% in Q4 2025 due to elevated AI investment. This is worth monitoring but does not suggest deterioration in demand.

The likely outcome here is not necessarily that AI causes a dramatic reduction in the workforce. More likely, seat-driven growth slows for these businesses as AI reduces the need for additional hiring across the industry. That does not change the necessity of MSCI's proprietary data.

Addressing the AI Concerns

In my view, the impact on the seat-based model does not lead to a dramatic reduction in revenue. Instead, MSCI appears well positioned for the shift from this model to a consumption-driven environment where MSCI's data is consumed by machine endpoints instead of humans.

First, I want to point out that MSCI is defending its seat-based model. The company is building AI into its Analytics suite, for example, through tools like AI Portfolio InsightsTools. This allows human users to interact with their portfolios using natural language, making humans more productive. This, in turn, should support retention and pricing power for the remaining licenses.

However, the shift from human seats to machine endpoints is happening. A growing portion of usage, particularly in the Analytics segment is being delivered through APIs and cloud platforms as opposed to through MSCI's applications. This shift is reflected in new business, including a seven-figure, multi-year deal to deliver equity models via Snowflake, which contributed 19% growth in new recurring subscription sales for Analytics.

A new monetization model does introduce some uncertainty. A consumption model is naturally more variable than a fixed subscription, which makes pricing power less clear in the near term. That said, I believe this shift will expand the monetization opportunity for MSCI's data and models. The reason being that instead of being limited to monetizing human users, MSCI can monetize every automated process that depends on its data and models.

Over time, I expect this to offset slower seat-driven growth, particularly because of data-hungry clients like hedge funds and market makers. Demand from these customers is primarily driven by data consumption, and they are more focused on data quality and throughput.

Ultimately, these efforts should be margin accretive. MSCI's increased investment in AI is driving automation across its workflows. This should reduce human involvement and lead to more scalable, lower-cost delivery of its data and models over time.

Custom Indexing: A Growth Driver

More specific to the index business, MSCI is leveraging AI to create custom indexes. What was historically a slow, manual process is now being deployed quickly and at scale using internal AI tools. Over the last five years, MSCI has licensed approximately 15,000 custom indexes, and $230 billion has flowed into exchange-traded products linked directly to them.

Going forward, I expect this to be an important growth lever for Index by expanding the AUM tied to MSCI's benchmarks. This effectively shifts the revenue mix toward ABF, which will help to offset pressure on seat-driven revenue. Since 2022, asset-based fee revenue from custom indexes has grown at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of over 20%.

The Supporting Cast: Sustainability and Private Assets

Sustainability/Climate and Private Assets represent about 20% of revenue combined. I view both as narrow-moat businesses with considerably more variability in potential outcomes than the Index business. Neither is the reason I own MSCI. They do, however, provide additional proprietary data that supports the core Index and Analytics segments. ESG data powers sustainability indexes, and Private asset benchmarks add to the offering that makes Analytics stickier.

Sustainability and Climate

Sustainability and Climate has seen its adjusted EBITDA margin more than double over the past five years to 36.3%. This is despite a dramatic deceleration in revenue growth and speaks to the internal AI-driven efficiencies MSCI is likely to benefit from across its business. The U.S. remains a genuine headwind because of its political environment, but EMEA demand appears to be a growth driver as regulatory disclosure requirements in Europe increase the value of ESG. I am also encouraged by the moves to broaden offerings for insurance and banking through GeoSpatial Asset Intelligence. This tool provides clients with more granular risk data made possible through AI automation.

Private Assets

Private Assets is earlier in its journey, as illustrated by the mid-20s EBITDA margins, but the segment strikes me as the most significant frontier for MSCI. Private AUM is projected to grow from $25 trillion in 2022 to $60 trillion or more by 2032. While 2025 revenue grew just 8.1%, net new subscription sales in Private Capital Solution (previously Burgiss) grew 86% in Q4.

The Burgiss data, which contains 40 years of cash flow data sourced directly from limited partners, is proprietary data and the competitive advantage for the segment. By integrating Burgiss and Vantager (which automates GP document processing), MSCI is positioning itself as the standardized infrastructure for an otherwise opaque market. Even without dominating the space, this segment adds value because owning this data likely makes MSCI a necessary part of the investment ecosystem.

Capital Allocation

MSCI currently has about $6.2 billion in debt and reports negative shareholders' equity of $2.65 billion. The negative equity is the result of debt being issued to fund share buybacks. In 2025, MSCI repurchased $2.4 billion in shares at an average price of approximately $560, shrinking the share count by roughly 5.4%. Over the past decade, the share count is down approximately 20%.

I do not necessarily love when companies fund capital returns with debt. It creates an unnecessary risk, but I suppose it speaks to management's confidence in their cash flows. MSCI converts 90%+ of net income to free cash flow, generates ROIC above 25%, and produces $1.46 billion in annual free cash flow. Total debt to adjusted EBITDA is 3.3x, within the company's intended target of 3.5x.

With about $2 billion of buyback authorization remaining and a solid history of dividend increases, capital returns likely remain a priority. It will be interesting to see if management maintains this pace of capital returns as the business transitions through a dynamic operating environment.

Valuation: Mispriced for Uncertainty

My DCF model implies an intrinsic value around $608 per share, representing a reasonable gap to today's market price. The stock is currently trading around 28x my 2026 adjusted earnings estimate. This is a premium multiple, justified by MSCI's compounding multiple, but low relative to its historic multiple in the high 30s.

My DCF supports this despite my assumptions leaning conservative relative to the quality of the company and its long-term opportunity. This can be seen in my terminal assumptions, where I use a discount rate of 7.5%, above MSCI WACC, and a terminal rate of only 3%.

On the top line, I assume a high single-digit CAGR over the next decade led by continued strength in the Index segment. This is supported by the continued shift toward passive investing and expected success of custom indexes. In Analytics, I assume mid-single-digit revenue growth reflecting the pivot in monetization. Growth is further supported by Sustainability and Private Assets, though with greater variability.

On the margin front, I expect only modest expansion from current levels, with operating margins reaching the high 50s over time. While current AI spending in Analytics is a near-term drag, I expect the shift toward machine endpoints and consumption-based pricing to drive operating leverage as the model scales.

Assuming reinvestment rises to around 3.5% of revenue, the model implies that free cash flow compounds around 9% over the next decade, reaching $3.3 billion by 2035.

Risks to the Thesis

There are several factors that could challenge assumptions. The shift to a consumption model is the most important unknown. Seat growth may slow faster than data consumption scales, or pricing power could weaken. In either case, revenue growth and margins would come under pressure.

This risk is compounded by increased AI spending. Sustaining EBITDA margins near current levels will require operating leverage to materialize as expected.

In the Index segment, fee compression is a threat. AUM growth has historically offset lower fees; however, sustained increases in pricing pressure could weigh on the compounding story. And while I view MSCI's data and models as difficult to replicate, more aggressive investment from large financial institutions and technology providers could eventually influence the pricing dynamics.

Lastly, MSCI's revenue is tied to equity markets. My model does not account for the impact of a sustained bear market.

Final Thoughts

In a world with AI, MSCI's competitive advantages remain intact. The technology does not change this, but it does change how the company is monetizing demand. The market appears to be pricing in the uncertainty of this transition by assigning a lower multiple to the stock. While some multiple compression is justified, MSCI remains essential financial infrastructure with exceptional fundamentals. In my view, this creates an opportunity to own a quality compounder at a more reasonable price.

data.title: Intel's Turnaround Gains Momentum
data.description:
By far, the most convincing argument in favor of a bullish outlook toward Intel Corporation (INTC) is that management has finally regained its execution discipline amid increasing structural demand for Intel chips amid the rise of AI applications and the opportunity of foundry optionality. Provided that 18A is successfully scaled up and external customers start converting orders, Intel will become not just a recovering semiconductor vendor but a strategic Western manufacturing platform with superior operating leverage and a profitability growth profile relative to the current consensus estimate.

Intel’s Problem Was Chaos; Now It’s Discipline, Not Hype

There will always be the risk of overcompensation with Intel. Thus, every new step taken by the company and every news item announced made it clear that the company had lost control over itself. With milestones finally being hit, the market is quick to claim that Intel is on the verge of a turnaround. While I do not subscribe to the former view, nor do I share the latter opinion. The changes that have occurred in operations within the company are very real and, in some ways, unexpected.

To my mind, the best evidence of the transformation process is that management is operating with an iron fist. In other words, Lip-Bu Tan is bringing Intel back to its financial essence after years of expansion in all directions. From reducing the workforce to slashing costs, from restraining the creation of more fab capacity to linking the 14A investments to client demands, Intel is doing what needs to be done. Yes, it may appear to be mundane, but Intel desperately required such a shift in focus.

On a separate note, the x86 business looks far healthier than the market believes. The client segment is not very exciting these days. However, it is still very monetizable. The Data Center segment is more interesting. According to management, demand for CPUs in terms of conventional server computing outpaces available supply amid rapidly expanding need for orchestration and control plane in the era of AI infrastructure.

While I have been inclined to agree with that view for some time, I do not think that it was a matter of opinion. Rather, it was a logical conclusion that AI applications would require more diversity rather than uniformity. Yet, it does not matter how strong the demand is if you fail to deliver.

Strong Demand Isn’t the Win; Intel Is Still Playing Catch-Up

To begin with, I think that Intel’s management did not properly assess the power of the demand for servers and their reliance on CPU technology in an ever-evolving world where there is much AI-based infrastructure. That is quite troubling because this suggests that the process of transforming the organization might be challenging.

Secondly, it seems quite likely that this is not just a one-time problem with the current level of production. Instead, Intel may find out that the demand for AI chips is structurally higher than expected. This becomes an issue because management should tighten its discipline and gain confidence as the market grows. This is already a challenging task, but in addition to that, it has to expand production at the same time.

Intel has the latest technologies for manufacturing. However, from its claims regarding a shortage of supplies, it becomes clear that Intel is not operating under conditions where it expands its capacities as per growing demand. Rather, it is struggling to catch up. That is why its turnaround is impressive, and that is precisely why it is more fragile.

The Foundry Dream is Compelling but the Economics Still Aren’t There

I keep coming back to the foundry business since this is where many market participants see massive upside for Intel. As soon as it gains credibility as a viable alternative for advanced node manufacturing in the Western Hemisphere, its potential skyrockets since the company will stop being just a recovering CPU maker and become a strategic manufacturing asset. I understand where the excitement comes from.

However, strategy is not equal to evidence. The fact of the matter is that Intel Foundry division remains loss-making, incurring an operating loss of $2.5 billion in Q4 2025. In Q4 2025, the foundry revenues from external customers amounted to $222 million. The advanced packaging division sounds promising, and judging by management commentary, it might be close to generating positive operating income from its operations.

Specifically, management is excited about EMIB technology and prepayment interest received from the customers. I take those comments seriously and understand why. Packaging technologies might be the first place to show that Intel has value as a manufacturer even if wafers are not yet profitable.

Yet, this is not enough. What Intel needs is not just technical credibility. What it needs is to prove that it can provide economically competitive services to its customers. I hope that yield improvement is progressing as described by management with a steady month-to-month increase. This is a good thing since it is a precondition to success. However, it is not enough, by definition. Intel still needs to become economically competitive and prove it to customers and the market.

Investor Valuation Assumptions Are Building on Top of One Another

My view on Intel valuation has shifted a bit. On a superficial level, it looks very appealing since a ~4.7x multiple in 2027 is reasonable for a company with strategic relevance in the area of AI infrastructure. However, as soon as I dive into the numbers and analyze earnings quality, I discover that a forward P/E of above 100x for 2026 is not based on growth expectations but rather a lack of earnings power.

To put it bluntly, investors are supposed to close the gap between the current lack of normalized profits and the expected earnings in 2027. This will lead to a doubling of EPS in 2027 with a P/E of around 50x. Given execution risks and continued negative FCF, such P/E is quite high. Therefore, while valuation is not undervalued, it is transitional. It assumes not only margin recovery but also foundry division improvement and sustainable demand.

Risks That Would Cause Me to Reframe

The first thing that might force me to reassess the narrative is if the 18A node proves to be viable technically but economically nonviable. In this case, the whole foundry business might need to be reassessed. The second risk would be if engagement with external customers remained at the talking stage without any material progress. In other words, if we see more interest and collaboration but no demand commitment from third-party customers, the market is bound to lose faith.

Currently, I have no doubt that Intel is right when it claims that server demand is high and growing. If this demand evaporates once supply is restored, then Intel's argument that AI requires CPUs might become less attractive for investment purposes. Finally, I should mention that negative FCF is a real risk. Intel keeps losing money and adding debt amid expansion in the foundry division.

Final Take

I no longer see Intel as a broken story. However, I still believe it remains an unfinished one. Turnaround is gaining momentum and becoming credible, which is a big achievement. So, INTC is a buy based on its improved execution and capital management, which are in sync with growing AI demand, setting up a huge earnings inflection for the company.

data.title: SPVM Could Keep Outperforming
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Value with Momentum ETF (SPVM), launched on 06/16/2011 and managed by Invesco Management LLC, provides exposure to S&P 500 stocks with value and momentum characteristics. It charges an expense ratio of 0.39% and manages ~$100 million.

SPVM's value/momentum exposure has underperformed the market in the long term, marked by an enduring bull run. However, the conditions in the last few months helped the fund start outperforming, and their likely persistence in the near term warrants a bias towards this ETF.

Methodology

SPVM tracks the S&P 500 High Momentum Value Index, which starts with the S&P 500 and ranks its constituents based on a value and momentum factor. Value is measured based on the book/price ratio, earnings/price ratio, and sales/price ratio. After the top 200 stocks by value are selected, they are ranked based on their momentum, defined by the TTM price performance, excluding the most recent month; the top 100 out of those based on momentum are selected.

Weighting is based on the value score, rather than the market capitalization of each constituent, making SPVM's approach more pure-value than those of many prominent large-cap value ETFs. The index is rebalanced semiannually, after the close on the third Friday of June and December.

Portfolio

The first thing that stands out below is the low idiosyncratic risk represented by the 17.4% exposure to the top 10 holdings. The top weight is also 2.6%, which shouldn't influence the overall performance to a meaningful degree in the short and long term. Another thing to keep in mind is that the top names reflect a tilt towards cyclical industries (Bunge, Ford, ADM, GM, Allstate, United Airlines; 11.42% of the portfolio).

This is also observed in the sector composition, as Financials, Consumer Cyclical, Energy, Industrials, and Basic Materials account for ~60% of the portfolio. However, I need to note that the fund has about 28% allocated in Utilities, Healthcare, and Consumer Defensive stocks; the S&P 500's exposure to those sectors is about 17%. This defensive sector bias can be useful in environments such as the one we find ourselves in.

Performance

SPVM has significantly underperformed the S&P 500 over the years due to its lack of a strong growth bias that underpinned the market's superior returns. It's well known that during persistent bull runs, value stocks underperform; the S&P 500 has been a growth-dominated index for a long time, with high exposure to technology stocks, and its market-cap-weighting approach only amplifies the growth bias.

Yes, SPVM is not just another value fund. However, momentum is only a secondary feature and should be thought of as operating within the value context, not on top of it. That's because the value screen is applied first, so the index doesn't necessarily have the highest-momentum stocks; only those value stocks that are strongest on momentum. Besides, the fund also weights based on a value score, amplifying the bias towards that factor.

If we go one step forward, it becomes even clearer why SPVM should not be expected to perform relatively well in a prolonged bullish environment. The usual hunting grounds for value stocks are the same sectors the ETF is mostly exposed to right now. More than one-third of the portfolio is in Financials, a highly regulated and capital-intensive sector, which should lag a long-duration growth sector like Information Technology when rates are low. Consumer Cyclical, Energy, Industrials, and Basic Materials are also highly cyclical sectors and tend to underperform tech stocks, which also enjoy secular growth drivers.

Now, SPVM's significant outperformance in the last six months has been driven by a rotation out of mega caps and growth stocks and into defensive options. This is a strong sign of broadening beyond tech stocks, which begs the question: Is it likely to persist?

Outlook

This broadening provides us with a very useful insight because the promising growth prospects of AI have been largely responsible for the market's impressive performance in the last few years. The broadening is also correlated with the market's recent reluctance to keep rewarding fast-growing mega caps like NVIDIA (NVDA) and Micron Technology (MU). Which is reasonable if you think that hyperscalers' CapEx for the AI buildout keeps growing during a period when the timing and magnitude of the ROI are unclear.

The war in Iran provides another issue for tech-concentrated funds, as it has caused a rise in inflation and interest rate expectations. This is a meaningful headwind for the tech sector, on which the market largely relies, while SPVM is less than 3% exposed to it right now.

Additionally, SPVM's allocation to cyclical sectors, as we already saw, is by no means low. However, its defensive exposure is higher than the market's. That's another relative tailwind for SPVM right now, due to an elevated stagflation risk.

Risks

The primary risk here is the uncertainty over how enduring the conditions that warrant value exposure are. If the war in Iran de-escalates quickly or, at least, the oil supply disruption is resolved, SPVM may underperform the market. First, its Energy exposure is more than double at 9.5%, making it vulnerable to an oil price crash, and, second, tech stocks could lead as they have been especially punished lately.

Conclusion

All in all, SPVM may not have performed well relative to the market over the long run, but its recent outperformance highlights its tactical use in periods like this. Its sector composition implies some risks, but it also hints at a potential persistence of its most recent outperformance, which currently outweighs them.

What do you think? Let's have a discussion in the comments. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Forget The Noise - I'm Preparing To Deploy More Capital Than Ever
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this year, I remember saying that this is one of the most challenging market environments in years. Not because I struggled financially, but because we were dealing with so many forces, including a very early (super fragile) economic rebound, massive AI disruption that caused almost daily headlines about new industries that could be on the chopping block, inflation uncertainty, and issues in areas like housing.

Then, the Iran War happened, and everything became even trickier, as it seems we get multiple escalation and de-escalation headlines every day, as the market is trying to figure out how long this war will take. And, to make matters worse, because the war involves major supply risks like the Strait of Hormuz, I have made the case that we’re in a binary market.

  • Either the war will be solved soon, and we can go back to the growth acceleration thesis that worked so well in January and February.
  • Or this war takes long enough that inflation spikes and causes the very fragile recovery to break, potentially triggering a recession that the market, which trades at 20x forward earnings, may not like (to put it mildly).

That’s basically the current market environment in a nutshell.

It’s either a very good outcome or a very bad outcome, and every day, we’re trying to figure out what the actual outcome will be. As I started writing this article after President Trump’s press conference, where he made clear he would hit Iran “extremely hard,” we’re once again dealing with a very weak market, as investors know the risks of something breaking in the economy are rising.

Trump signaled a potential escalation in military action, saying the U.S. could intensify strikes over the next two to three weeks and warning Iran would be pushed “back to the stone ages.”

At the same time, he left the door open to diplomacy, saying talks are ongoing. However, he cautioned that if no deal is reached, the U.S. could “hit each and every one of their electric generating plants very hard and probably simultaneously.” - Seeking Alpha.

And the interesting thing is that while I am writing this, S&P 500 futures (the famous e-mini) are trading just 7% below their all-time high. We could drop another 300 basis points, and we would still barely be in a correction. I have to be honest, market sentiment feels different.

I believe that’s because of two reasons.

  • Because this is a “binary” market environment, the market is nervous because it knows what could happen if things go south in the economy.
  • The S&P 500 (SP500) is top-heavy, which means a lot more weakness is happening under the hood that is remaining hidden if we only assess the index with 40% exposure to its biggest ten holdings. As I have written before, in January and February, that top-heavy characteristic was negative. Now, it’s positive for index holders.

With all of this in mind, as the title already gave away, I’m not bearish. On the contrary. I am preparing to deploy the most cash ever (yes, really) in the months ahead, as I have been accumulating cash for months. I’ll move to Albania later this month (assuming I do not run into new obstacles), and I am launching Main Street Alpha with my partner, Albert Marko, on April 7 on Seeking Alpha.

Interestingly enough, while none of us like the market’s weakness (I obviously preferred the massive uptrend in my portfolio in January and February), we’re both excited that the market is offering us new opportunities to invest.

That’s what this article is all about, as I’ll tell you what goes on between my ears, and how I’m looking to invest.

So, let’s get to it!

Why The Market Is So Nervous

I just gave you the situation in a nutshell. But as this is Seeking Alpha and my job is to give you the full story, that obviously isn’t enough.

One big reason why the market is nervous is that there seems to be no middle ground. We’re either going back to normal (so to speak) or the major supply and energy issues push us into a recession. As the market trades at a lofty valuation, odds are that this could lead to a much bigger correction than we’re currently witnessing.

It’s all theoretical, but that’s how I see it.

And I’m not alone.

As Marcus Ashworth wrote for Bloomberg (he’s the chief markets strategist for Haiton Securities), supply chains are a major issue, especially because factors like inflation work their way through the economy. I think we all remember how big that effect was after the pandemic. Inflation was everywhere, and, so far, it’s still not at 2%.

Basically, the thesis has shifted in a way that major players on Wall Street expect inflation to lead to rather rapid demand destruction, if we were to see a worsening situation.

Several major bond fund managers, including JPMorgan Chase & Co. and Columbia Threadneedle Investments, reckon that higher bond yields this month are misjudging the risks of a slow down, according to Bloomberg News. “What tends to begin as an inflation shock can quickly migrate into a growth shock, and we are on the cusp of seeing a significant weakening in the economy,” Daniel Ivascyn, the chief investment officer of Pacific Investment Management Co., told Bloomberg. Demand destruction is the typical outcome of higher energy prices. This is already underway in the oil market, as my colleague Javier Blas has highlighted. - Bloomberg

Right now, according to the same Bloomberg article, expectations are for 4.2% inflation. It would be 220 basis points above the Fed’s target and basically eliminate all hopes for further cuts, in a market that was just about to bet on higher growth and easing pressure on housing.

With that in mind, the five-year forward inflation swap, which is the expected inflation rate over the next five years (it can be traded and used as a hedging tool), is barely above 2.3%, as it has dropped during the Iran War. That’s weird, isn’t it? After all, we just discussed how inflation is a risk.

The reason is demand destruction. To give you a silly example, if oil were to rise to $200, it may be inflationary, but it would be so expensive that demand would suffer. That could trigger a chain reaction that ends up being disinflationary on a longer-term basis.

In other words, the chart above perfectly displays economic growth fears.

This is exactly the kind of environment where I want to buy.

The Bullish Thesis Remains Strong

So far, the bad news. As I have written before, I am very much aware of these headwinds. That’s why I give them so much attention. Yet, I also made clear that this isn’t causing me to change my thesis, as I stick to the cyclical value thesis/rotation.

In most Big Picture articles, I used the risks segment at the end to explain that growth fears could trigger a “counter-rotation” back into large-cap growth stocks - at least on a relative basis. That has happened so far.

Yet I don’t think that will last.

I truly believe that underlying growth in the U.S. is strong enough to withstand current Iran headwinds (assuming things do not escalate) and bounce back even harder once these risks settle.

That’s based on leading indicators. For March, for example, the leading ISM Manufacturing Index actually improved further. It hit the highest level since 2022. That’s despite a full month of war-related headlines.

Although I try to refrain from making short-term predictions, I had expected the ISM Index to remain in growth territory (above 50), yet decline a bit compared to February, simply due to the new risks that come with the war in Iran.

However, the index rose to 52.7 despite the fact that more than 60% of all comments were negative. 20% were negative due to tariffs. 40% were negative due to the war.

Among the positive comments was machinery:

“Customer orders have increased considerably as the construction market remains strong, resulting in higher production volume and increased forecasts to suppliers.” - ISM (Via Bloomberg).

When adding the red-hot inflation indicator, there are two things I’m incorporating in my research:

  • Inflation is about to run hot and likely work its way through the economy, similar to the first year of the Ukraine War.
  • The U.S. economy remains very robust.

This is also what we have written for Main Street Alpha (coming to Seeking Alpha), as we believe that the government is actively “running it hot,” by accepting higher inflation to kickstart growth and a beneficiary of the Iran War in a way that it benefits American dominance in areas like energy, agriculture, and long-term economic re-shoring.

In other words, inflation is now a feature, as it acts as the necessary "lube" to devalue the debt in real terms while keeping the nominal economic engine roaring. This is the essence of the "Quiet QE" we have been covering for months. The government is essentially injecting massive liquidity and stimulus through fiscal channels and regulatory easing, rather than relying solely on traditional central bank bond-buying.

On top of that, the ongoing conflict in Iran has introduced a severe oil shock to the global system. This is systematically isolating emerging markets and pressuring energy competitors. Recent macroeconomic outlooks are already warning that this oil shock could push headline inflation back toward the 4% mark in the near term, and even that would be a somewhat low base case, if you ask me. - Main Street Alpha

This is obviously a tricky thesis, both for me as an investor and for the government, as it needs to balance growth and inflation. The Iran War makes this much harder.

Yet I think it will work. If I didn’t believe that, I would likely end up buying a lot of gold and exiting equities, yet as I’m a long-term investor, I think we’re looking at the convergence of rebounding cyclical growth, long-term tailwinds in economic re-shoring, and rising momentum in areas like housing.

I’m buying in all of these areas.

What I’m Looking To Buy

The tricky thing about making plans is the volatile nature of the market.

Initially, in February, I was almost forced to add new investments, as the majority of my portfolio had surged in value. Now, I am not necessarily forced to do that, as a lot of cyclical value stocks in my portfolio are on sale.

This includes cyclical transportation stocks with economic re-shoring tailwinds like Union Pacific (UNP), Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), and Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL). I could add exposure to all of these in a heartbeat.

Then there’s QXO (QXO). Like many housing-related stocks, it has suffered a lot in recent weeks. Although it’s still a top-4 position for me, I’m very eager to add to this position. If I had a bigger portfolio (meaning more individual stocks), I would also buy Builders FirstSource (BLDR) at these levels.

And then there are the asset managers. As I have written before, I am a very cyclical portfolio. I want to diversify in areas that are underrepresented. This includes Ares Management (ARES) in private credit and Brookfield Corp. (BN) in private equity.

Both stocks would look very good in my portfolio. And, right now, BN would have my preference. While I believe that ARES is cheaper, BN is less volatile and would have greater diversification benefits for my portfolio.

In tech, I am looking at Amazon (AMZN), which is my favorite Mag-7 stock in this market, mainly due to its ability to grow margins using AI and being somewhat insulated from the war for the best AI model. Alphabet (GOOGL) is also on my radar, yet after last year’s rally and the impressive innovation in models like Claude, it’s not my favorite anymore.

Other honorable mentions include Kinsale Capital (KNSL), which is a specialized insurance company I’ve been researching a lot more, as it has leading operating ratios, a terrific in-house operating system, and strong pricing power in a market that many insurance companies avoid.

Needless to say, over the next 1-3 months, I’ll likely turn a lot of these plans into reality, as it’s time to deploy capital again.

And despite the market’s headwinds, I cannot tell you how excited I am for all of this.

Takeaway

The headlines are scary, and I truly think this is a “binary” market.

However, I’m not bearish, but looking to deploy more cash than ever. Despite the risks, I think this is a market with a lot of hidden value, especially once the market focuses on the underlying cyclical recovery that started earlier this year. Despite the tricky first weeks of the war, that sentiment is still bullish, which means I return to my core ideas, which are broadening growth, re-shoring, and the “run it hot” thesis.

It also means that I’ll provide a lot more food for thought going forward and updates on my thesis.

Stay tuned!

data.title: Element Solutions: A Lot Of Good News Arrives
data.description:
Shares of Element Solutions (ESI) have seen healthy gains as of late, with investors approving of the acquisition streak that the company is undergoing. The company recently closed on two acquisitions, set to drive meaningful growth this year, warranting an update on the situation here.

Recent dealmaking takes place at very reasonable valuation multiples and strongly adds to the growth profile of Element, both of which get my thumbs up. Moreover, the deal-making efforts come at a time when Element itself is seeing stronger growth, as this good news show has rightfully sent shares (and multiples) higher here.

Following these gains, most of the immediate attraction to the risk-reward proposition seems to have been gone. However, I am happy to provide continued coverage on Element here and actively willing to consider buying on dips.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Adding to the Business

In January, Element Solutions closed on the acquisition of EFC Gases & Advanced Materials, a deal that was announced in November of last year. The company acquired the high-purity specialty gases and other advanced materials provider at 12 times anticipated adjusted EBITDA in 2026. Its solutions are supplied to high-end industries such as semiconductor manufacturing, aerospace, and electrical infrastructure.

With the deal seen adding $30 million in EBITDA next year, the implied purchase price comes in at $360 million. As EBITDA margins are seen exceeding 30%, the implied sales contribution approaches $100 million, with revenues having grown at a compounded annual growth rate of about 15% over the past 17 years. Given a 40% exposure to semiconductor markets and 30% exposure to defense/aerospace, all of this looks quite appealing.

This deal was followed by the closing of the deal for Micromax, which took place early in February. That $500 million transaction was announced in October, marking an opportunistic acquisition to buy the asset from struggling and overleveraged peer Celanese (CE). These assets focus on the supply of advanced electric inks and pastes used in harsh environments in similar end markets, including aerospace, defense, and healthcare. Those activities were bought at similar 12.5 times EBITDA multiples, suggesting a $40 million contribution on that front.

A Solid 2025 and Eventful 2026

In February, Element reported its 2025 results. Reported revenues rose by 4% to $2.55 billion, with organic growth reported at 6% following a divestment early in the year. This was driven by a strong end to the year, with fourth-quarter organic growth rates reported at 10%.

Growth was driven by a 10% increase in organic sales for the electronics business, that segment making up 70% of total sales. Specialties organic growth rates were mostly flat for the year, as both segments posted fairly similar segment margins in the low twenties.

Adjusted earnings for the year rose by just five cents to $1.49 per share, with fourth-quarter earnings up two cents to $0.37 per share. Note that sales advancements surpassed the earnings advancement. The company ended the year with exactly a billion in net debt, which is perfectly manageable in relation to a $548 million EBITDA number reported for the year, resulting in a 1.8 times leverage ratio.

Accelerating momentum made that share rise to the $34 mark, as 242 million shares granted Element an $8.2 billion equity valuation, for a $9.2 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 17 times EBITDA, with equity trading around 23 times adjusted earnings.

That, however, is a bit misleading given the recent M&A executed and the outlook provided. Recent dealmaking came at a combined $869 million deal tag, seen boosting EBITDA by around $70 million. This makes for a pro forma net debt load of nearly $1.9 billion. Fortunately, 2026 EBITDA is seen up to $650-$670 million, which, if we strip out the M&A contribution, suggests an organic $580-$600 million EBITDA guidance for 2026, marking solid organic growth. Pro forma leverage is seen around 2.8 times if we factor in the most recent deals.

While the reconciliation to earnings has not been provided, we can make some assumptions. Based on adjusted earnings around $1.50 per share, we see $100-$120 million in additional EBITDA. Amidst minimal depreciation intensity, an estimated $50 million in incremental interest costs, and tax rates, I see a post-tax earnings contribution around $45 million, driven by M&A and organic growth.

That is equivalent to nearly $0.20 per share, which reveals a roadmap for earnings to advance a long way to $1.70 per share, which, based on a price around $34 per share, implies a 20 times forward earnings multiple.

And Now?

Since the fall, when I concluded that Element was resorting to M&A to complement stronger organic achievements, I was turning more upbeat on the business and prospects for the shares. With the business having a long and rich corporate history, Element has been a stable, or stagnant, business. However, the company was improving its business focus and saw improved organic growth, as a $26 stock looked reasonably valued at the time, with shares trading around 17-18 times anticipated earnings.

With growth becoming more sustainable and structural, I was turning more upbeat on Element here, and, frankly, shares have been on a tear ever since. In fact, shares have risen some 30% to current levels at $34 per share, driven by a solid end to 2025 and comforting guidance for 2026, which now includes another deal.

In between all this, I like the progress made, yet the re-rating has been quite substantial as well, as a near 3 times leveraged business has seen its earnings multiple increase to about 20 times here. This makes me naturally turn a bit more cautious here, also given the additional leverage incurred.

data.title: Bank OZK: Part Of The Regional Banking Undervaluation, I Say 'BUY'
data.description:
I used to cover Bank OZK (OZK) when it still held what I view to be a far superior name, Bank of the Ozarks. I cannot think why they abandoned this name, though a name, of course, isn't enough for me to "mark a company down" as such. I had been out of the bank for quite some time when I sold it above $50/share. I have not had cause to regret my course of action when doing so, given that for the last 4-5 years, the bank has failed to materialize any higher sort of upside.

While of course it would be the height of hubris for me to suggest that I foresaw this anemia, I will say that at the time I divested, there was a risk that there was going to be a "flatter" sort of performance from the company's fundamentals and potentials.

However, recently I have "gotten into" US regional banks again, and started investing. If you know me and my investment style, you know that there is only one reason why I move into banks like this again, especially during what I believe to be an economic downturn - and that is, dear readers, value for my money.

I'm a value-oriented investor. That means I only buy things that I get cheaply relative to what I believe to be their fair value.

So, what about OZK? Has the time come to give this company a PT that would suggest an upside here?

I believe this is the case, though I also believe the upside is not as high as some analysts would suggest here. When it comes to banks, we always need to look at what makes up their assets and where they get their outperformance potential from. I recently covered Huntington (HBAN) which I consider to be a stellar regional bank with a lot of potential. I have also invested in the bank. OZK is not as good as HBAN, as I see things, but I still believe it to have upside.

So let's see what we have here.

Bank OZK - Upside from regional banking

So, the bullish thesis for OZK is pretty well-established by SA analysts other than me, at the time of publishing. The average here is a "STRONG BUY". But this might be too simplified a picture - Wall Street may be at "BUY", but Quant is at "HOLD", citing challenges in areas like revisions and momentum. So, there is likely at the very least some data that is telling a different story than the bullish one here.

The bullish scenario and outlook are based on the diversification of its loan book with strong capital reserves, its valuation, and the yield of its preferred shares. The prefs aren't the subject of this article - what I will look at are the trading level and fundamentals of the common shares.

Since I last covered the company, a few things have changed. The company is still primarily an east-south bank in the US, with its base in Arkansas. They now have assets of over $40B, loans of $32.B, and deposits of $33B+. On that basis, things seem solid enough. An NII of about 4.3% is solid, an efficiency ratio of 35.6% is so-so, and overall, the finances are looking good. The company has been growing its assets, loans, deposits, NI, net income, and EPS. All has been good. On this basis, I can certainly see where some of the appeal and the bullish considerations come from.

The company has, since ZIRP, been able to increase its overall yield returns while lowering its investment balances overall. NII for OZK has been consistently better than the FDIC average, but this is the case with many (and most) regional banks, because they tend to have somewhat different risk profiles than standard banks. This is also why they tend to trade at lower multiples, and why I believe OZK may be by some investors a bit "overestimated".

Overall, I consider OZK's balance sheet and fundamentals to be solid. But when it comes to regional banks, there are a few differentiators that I consider to be valid when looking at what makes a good and what makes a great regional bank. One of the ways I differentiate regional banks is by looking at some of their riskier exposure and asset risk, and one of the asset classes I use for differentiation is CRE exposure. CRE exposure is a useful metric for comparing regional banks specifically, because for many of the regionals, it makes up a significant portion of their debt - that is not the case at larger banks, which are usually much better diversified than the regional ones.

So because we want to be risk-conservative, we want to focus on regional banks that might not be overexposed to it. Overexposure means that in the case of a downturn, which has happened before, the overexposed banks are more likely than not to see quite a bit of downturn and pressure.

The reason that OZK is one bank that, unfortunately, raises a few flags on my part is that the company has a significant exposure to CRE. When looking at these banks, metrics like CRE to equity start mattering more. Regulators tend to flag banks that have an excess of CRE loans (usually 300% of their capital). OZK is unfortunately much deeper into CRE at this time.

So, to make this clear, the main danger and main risk to OZK at any one time now is the company's exposure to loans that have to do with commercial real estate. I have been looking at this risk in some of my previous articles, and the bank's risk ratio has not improved (to that effect, in the last few years). As it stands, the company's loan portfolio is over 54% exposed to construction loans. Even as of late, write-offs have continued to be a part of this part of its loan exposures. Recently, in 4Q25, the company suffered a $72M write-off. The point is, OZK's exposure to these types of CRE loans is significant, and unlike other banks, it's unlikely to change going forward.

The assertion by bank management that their loans are supported by low LTV ratios and focus on what are considered high-quality construction projects. This, however, is not something that I would consider convincing, because those value ratios are determined not relative to market crashes. Even if the company were to bring the RESG segment down to below 50%, I wouldn't consider it to be any less risky than at slightly over 50%. It simply is part of the bank's characteristics.

The company's 4Q results saw record net income, but I'd rather emphasize the company's continued CRE-related construction loans. There's a rise in NPLs, there are rises in credit provisions, and there are more potential downsides from its office/lab projects.

Some of the company's credit costs missed expectations and forecasts by over 30% during the quarter. We're also at a point in time where we can identify individual higher-risk CRE loans. The company does have stellar credit ratios and return ratios, but given its amount of variable-rate CRE loans, it would, in fact, be very worrying if this were not the case.

The company needs exactly these fundamentals if it were to go through a recession, because in a recession, what could happen to its loan portfolio is not a pretty picture. Looking at historical recessions and the impact they have on CREs, using both GFC and COVID-19 estimates, the company's portfolio is likely to see the following trends.

First, it's likely to see an even higher charge-off amount - increased non-accruals, particularly with its larger office and life science sectors.

While the company's LTV of 40-50% and CET-1% are likely to help, the history we're comparing it to could see the bank lose 10-20% in the form of volume decline, requiring significant provisions for higher loan losses and the like.

It is true that even during the GFC, the company's NCOs (charge-offs, net) were in fact lower than its peers, but this doesn't mean that it won't impact the company's fundamentals or portfolio.

To simplify, what I am saying is the following. OZK is a great bank, but it's built over 50% on CRE loans. This does not make it uninvestable in any way, shape, or form - but it does require discounting. The bullish investors to whom I alluded earlier seem to put the company at a sector-average banking P/E. I find that to not be justifiable, given the exposure this company has. I don't believe OZK should be valued similarly to a bank that has less than 15% CRE exposure.

That is what guides my valuation approach for the bank - the CRE portfolio - and given that valuations typically can fall anywhere from 10-35%, I don't view the company's conservative LTV as a complete safety here.

Bank OZK - A bank that knows what it does, but with a fair bit of valuation uncertainty

Larger banks tend to see P/E ratios of 11-13x. Regional banks tend to work at similar levels, the low teens, with some at 10x and some closer to 12-13x. I value based on underlying asset quality, yield coverage, management, size, and efficiency. I gave HBAN a very high valuation and a premium to other regionals because its asset portfolio, lack of CRE exposure, and size, as well as well-covered yield, gave it plenty of justification.

OZK receives, unfortunately, no such forbearance from me.

Trading at 7.5x P/E, it's hard to say that OZK is not undervalued. I believe also that it is. The question is, is at what price target should it be considered undervalued? To a 13x P/E? That would put it at peer levels, and I would consider that wrong on every level. With less than a 4% yield even now, and a 50%+ RESG exposure, the company is not of that nature. But it's not just CRE - the bank is active in construction loans, which I consider to be materially riskier than normal CRE loans. This is because the underlying collateral doesn't even exist yet, which means not only the typical asset risk, but cost overruns, operational risks, potential abandonment of projects - all of that stuff. Now, OZK obviously knows how to manage risk - its history clearly shows us this.

So I'm willing to give the company a bit of a break - but I'm unwilling to go too far. I believe the Quant "note of caution" is warranted. The bulls are too positive here, but I believe the bears are too negative.

What I did when valuing the company's RESG portfolio, which is about 54.4% of total loans, which, based on total loans, comes to somewhere in the $15-$18B range. I then stress-tested these loans and repayments under GFC-like conditions. It helps a lot that the company's market cap, of about $5B, is materially lower than the value of its loans. The market is already doing much of our work for us.

What OZK doesn't get from me is consideration for its attempts to diversify. You don't get a mark for trying to do something. I will revisit the bank's valuation when it does it.

It's clear that this is also a worry that many people see. OZK has not only underperformed the market, but it has also underperformed its own actual AEPS growth for going on 2-5 years at this point.

It's clear from the share price movements that there are "attempts" at breaking through, which are then stopped by shorting or by investors selling into profit. Looking at it historically, it's almost as though you can see the company's share price ceiling in its current state, which seems to come to about $52-$53/share.

So what does this share price imply?

It means that the market believes a 9.5x P/E is as high as OZK is going to get. It means that the bank is essentially being discounted by the market by as much as 15-25% to peers. I do not view this way of valuing the stock as invalid, but I would allow the company a slightly better upside. Owing to some changes, some growth, and some estimates for double-digit upside into 2027-2028, I would be willing to give OZK a PT of about $47/share. That would make it barely a buy here, but still one.

I want to convey to you that the PT of $47/share puts me in the bearish camp for the company's RESG valuation. A bullish valuation of it, owing to its LTV, is entirely possible to go up to upwards of $80/share in terms of fair value - more than double the $40/share I believe the company to be at the very least worth.

Once RESG accounts for less than 45% of loans, I'd be willing to allow the company a higher multiple, but for now, the risk overhang, coupled with the better alternative that I believe HBAN represents, makes me a "BUY" rating with a very slim upside.

Thesis

Bank OZK is a fundamentally attractive regional bank - but I don't think it's as good as some of the bulls would have you believe, nor as risky as the bearish assertions would have you value it. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $47/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is now barely doing. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "BUY" rating. Given the share price, I say that we are now in a position where we can invest in this business.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $47/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Broadcom: Multi-Year Growth Visibility For Sale At ~0.5x PEG
data.description:
AVGO stock: FQ1 2026 earnings reveal multi-year visibility

My last work on Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) was published on Feb 24. That article focused on the options (put options in particular) for the stock and ended with a buy rating. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around AVGO both in terms of macroeconomics and also company specifics. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on updates provided in its FQ1 2026 earnings report (ER). In a nutshell, I see years of profit and growth visibility for the company from its FQ1 ER. With the combined effects of its updated growth potential and the valuation correction amid the broader market’s ongoing volatility, I also believe this potential is priced at a discounted price, as reflected by a PEG (P/E growth) ratio far below 1x. As such, I keep seeing a skewed return/risk curve and maintain my buy rating.

Let me start with a very brief recap of the ER released on March 4. As seen in the snapshot below, Broadcom demonstrated another quarter of consistent top- and bottom-line expansion in FQ1. Revenue has scaled significantly from $14.92B in FQ1 2025 to $19.31B in the past quarter, representing a robust year-over-year (YOY) growth rate of almost 30%. This revenue acceleration is mirrored in the bottom-line too, as EPS rose from $1.60 in the prior year's Q1 to $2.05 in the last quarter, representing a YOY growth rate of more than 28%.

AVGO stock: growth-adjusted valuation

With the updated growth curve and the share price changes since the ER, Broadcom currently presents a valuation profile that appears very attractive either in absolute terms or relative to close peers. As an example, the chart below compares its FWD P/E ratios and PEG ratios to the Magnificent Seven stocks (with TSLA excluded). As seen, with a non-GAAP FY1 P/E of 27.72x, AVGO’s raw multiple is well aligned with the rest of this group of overachievers. It is below Apple's 30x P/E, in-line with AMZN’s 27x P/E, and slightly above GOOG’s 25.5x P/E. It carries a premium over the rest of the group, whose readings range from about 22x to 19x.

The valuation becomes even more attractive when adjusted for growth. Due to its superior growth potential, AVGO’s P/E is projected to compress significantly to only 17.8x and 14.2x for FY2 and FY3, offering some of the most attractive multiples among this group. In terms of growth-adjusted PEG ratios (on a non-GAAP and FWD basis), AVGO’s reading is somewhere between 0.67 and 0.45 as seen, not only substantially below the 1x threshold most GARP investors consider as the gold standard but also among the most attractive levels among the Mag 7 stocks.

AVGO stock: growth catalysts and growth visibility

More specifically, following the ER, consensus EPS estimates for Broadcom project significant expansion through the end of the decade. As shown in the chart below, the EPS estimate of $11.35 for full FY 2026 reflects a 66% YoY growth rate. This momentum continues into FY 2027 with a forecasted EPS of $17.84 and a YOY rate of 57%. At the current forward P/E of 27.72x, the implied PEG ratio for FY 2026 and 2027 would be in the range of 0.42x and 0.48x only.

And I do see plenty of growth catalysts that can fuel the above growth projections, judging by the updates provided in the FQ1 ER. In particular, I see multi-year growth visibility given the updates on its customers and product mix (see the ER slide below as an example). Two top catalysts on my list include the addition of a 6th hyperscaler as its customer and also the custom ASICs’ inherent nature. The new customer, when combined with Meta’s XPU projects (which was reaffirmed in the ER), should secure a large chunk of AVGO’s demand for years to come. In the meantime, custom ASICs inherently offer a multi-year design cycle in my experience, which again helps to offer multi-year visibility to most, if not all, of AVGO’s key customer programs. The Google TPU project probably is the best example here, with details offered in the following report:

Inside Google's Ironwood TPU v7 Supply Chain. Broadcom has been Google’s primary ASIC implementation partner across all TPU generations. For Ironwood, Broadcom handles the translation of Google’s architectural specifications into manufacturable silicon: physical design, SerDes IP for the high-speed ICI and external interfaces, and the full tape-out process with TSMC. The architecture and IP belong to Google, but Broadcom does the heavy lifting of making it manufacturable at the required process nodes and packaging configurations.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks, both on the downside and upside, are worth mentioning. On the downside, the company’s capex remains elevated to fuel the expected growth. To wit, capital expenditure was more than $8.7 billion in the last quarter, as seen in the next chart, far above the historical average in recent years. As another downside risks, software growth continued a deceleration trend. While the semiconductor segment surged, the Infrastructure Software segment (largely VMware) kept showing signs of cooling in the past quarter. Looking ahead, new enterprise AI tools, from companies like Anthropic targeting legacy code modernization, pose a fundamental threat to disrupt VMware’s core value proposition in my mind.

The comment above regarding its capex also reminds me to point out that the accounting P/E ratios quoted above tend to overstate the valuation ratios of high-growth stocks like AVGO. AVGO’s aggressive capex in recent quarters has been largely growth-oriented, while its maintenance capex is relatively low. To wit, the chart below also shows its Total Depreciation and Amortization (D&A). As a very crude estimate, if we use the D&A to approximate its maintenance capex, it is only $773 million in the past quarter, less than 10% of its total capex. To get an alternative estimate for cross checking purposes, I also followed Bruce Greenwald’s method (as detailed in his book entitled Value Investing) to delineate its growth and maintenance capex. The growth part of the capex is optional and thus should be considered part of the owner’s earnings (OE) following the wisdom of Warren Buffett. After adjusting for the growth portion of the capex, the Greenwald method revealed to me that AVGO’s OE has been on average ~19% higher than its accounting EPS in recent years. Thus, its P/E ratios based on owner’s earnings would be lower, which in turn leads to an even more attractive PEG ratio too.

To close, this AVGO revisit was largely motivated by two new catalysts since my last writing: the business updates provided in its FQ1 2026 ER and also the valuation changes amid the ongoing market volatility. I believe the broader volatility has distracted investors and resulted in a mispricing of AVGO’s growth potential. As a reflection, the PEG ratio is heavily discounted under current conditions, and the discount is even more unusual when the growth visibility ahead is considered.

data.title: W. P. Carey: Not Bad, But Not Great Either
data.description:
Nearly a year ago, in May of 2025, I wrote my last article about W. P. Carey (WPC). If you're not familiar with the business, that's understandable. However, it is a rather large REIT that has a market capitalization as of this writing of $15.21 billion and an enterprise value of $23.30 billion. That makes it a pretty large player in a large but fragmented space. In that article, I acknowledged that the company was exhibiting strength. This was thanks to certain growth initiatives that management had been employing. But the valuation of the company looked only decent. And at the end of the day, this led me to call it a ‘hold’ candidate.

Whenever I assign a company a ‘hold’ rating, I am asserting my view that shares should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. Since then, the stock has outperformed some, generating a return for investors of 13.9%. But that's not drastically above the 11.1% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. The newest data that investors have access to at the moment shows some rather impressive strength on the top line for the business. But bottom-line results have been mixed. This year, we can expect some expansion as management spends a significant amount of capital on investment opportunities. But when you compare the enterprise to other similar firms, it's not quite appealing enough, in my view, to justify an upgrade.

Checking Back in on W. P. Carey

One of the beautiful things about REITs is that they offer investors a wide array of different assets to buy into. Some companies that are structured this way focus on data centers. Others focus on retail assets. Others focus on office assets. Other examples exist as well. You also have enterprises that might have a specific geographic focus. It's difficult, I would argue, to have an investment preference that no REIT exists to satisfy. When it comes to W. P. Carey specifically, the company describes itself as a leading owner of commercial real estate, with the assets that it owns spread between the US and Europe. All told, the company had 1,682 properties in its portfolio at the end of 2025. These were all net-leased to its 371 tenants spread across 25 different countries. Although it is geographically diverse, it is still a US-centric business. 61% of its annualized base rent last year came from the US, with 33% attributable to properties spread throughout various European nations.

The 183.5 million square feet that the company has in its portfolio caters to a wide array of industries. In the two images above, for instance, you can see its exposure to its 25 largest tenants. We have a lot of variety here, including self-storage companies, pharmaceutical research and development and manufacturing properties, health and fitness facilities, automotive parts manufacturing properties, private schools, distribution facilities, retail assets, and more. In fact, W. P. Carey is one of the more diverse REITs out there, giving investors a taste of almost everything.

When you break up the data into property types, it is true that there is greater concentration. As an example, about 38.4% of its properties by annualized base rent involve industrial assets. But we also have 25.2% tied up in warehouses, 22.4% in retail, and 14.1% in other miscellaneous properties like education facilities, self-storage units, laboratories, hotels, and even land.
